title: BIOS Policy Compliance (Cisco UCS)
exclusions: Coverage focuses on UCS Manager inventory MO classes under `cisco:ucs:inventory` for biosUnit, biossettings, and the biosVf family, joined to `lsServer` for service profile name and tier heuristics. Intersight-primary X-class estates whose BIOS policy intent is not represented in a UCSM inventory feed you index are excluded from this data path—see UC-19.1.20 and UC-19.1.21 for bundle and HCL style hygiene when the control plane is not classic UCSM. Standalone C-series on CIMC only, with no UCSM session, is excluded; there is no `cisco:ucs:inventory` stream. lsServer `type` values that end in `template` (template objects, not a bound instance) are excluded from the join, which is the same idea as the sibling profile UC and prevents lab templates from faking a fleet count. Transient `applying` and `pending-reboot` `configState` classes that read like drift in the minutes after a policy push, when the true outcome is a scheduled reboot, are excluded at triage, not with a naïve daily mute.
evidence: Saved report or alert `uc_19_1_17_bios_token_compliance` on `index=cisco_ucs` plus a weekly CSV export of the `table` stage with `sp_name`, `token_name`, `current_value`, `expected_value`, and `policy_note` stored in a locked object store for the compliance owner.
dataSources: Primary: `index=cisco_ucs` and sourcetype `cisco:ucs:inventory` from the Splunk Add-on for Cisco UCS (`Splunk_TA_cisco_ucs`, [Splunkbase 2731](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/2731), 4.3.x). Each poll event carries a `classId` (for example biosVfIntelVirtualizationTechnology, biosVfAesNi, or biossettings) and a `dn` that anchors under the physical path `sys/chassis-.../blade-...` or `sys/rack-unit-.../bios/...`. The effective token value is usually in `vpEnable` and/or `vpValue` with occasional vendor renames, so the primary SPL `coalesce()`-wraps the common spellings. Per-token BIOS policies are a large family: include `biosVfPwrPerfTuning`, `biosVfMemoryRas`, `biosVfSubNumaCluster`, `biosVfBootMode`, `biosVfPatrolScrub`, and the rest your platform team lists in the baseline, not a single synthetic field. A separate pull on the same index still uses `cisco:ucs:inventory` with `classId=lsServer` to read `pnDn` for profile association, because there is not a second `cisco:ucs:config` sourcetype in the canonical add-on for service profile state; inventory is the supported pattern here too.
app: `Splunk_TA_cisco_ucs` ([Splunkbase 2731](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/2731), v4.3.x, Splunk-supported on Enterprise 9.2+ and Splunk Cloud). The on-disk app id is `Splunk_TA_cisco_ucs` (underscores); older install bundles may still show a hyphen in the file name only.
spl:
`comment("UC-19.1.17 — BIOS Policy Compliance (Cisco UCS): per-server per-token biosUnit/biossettings/biosVf* INVENTORY MO values vs ucs_bios_policy_baseline, joined to lsServer for sp_name. Educational: a templated cisco:ucs:bios sourcetype is not shipped in Splunk_TA_cisco_ucs — emit BIOS through cisco:ucs:inventory and classId.")`
index=cisco_ucs sourcetype="cisco:ucs:inventory" earliest=-24h@h
| eval class_id=lower(coalesce('classId', classId, ""))
| where class_id="biosunit" OR class_id="biossettings" OR like(class_id, "biosvf%")
| where isnotnull(dn) AND match(dn, "^sys/")
| eval server_mo=if(match(dn, "/[Bb]ios"), replace(dn, "/[Bb]ios.*$", ""), null())
| where isnotnull(server_mo) AND server_mo!=""
| eval token_name=coalesce('classId', classId, "")
| eval e_enable=coalesce('vpEnable', vpEnable, vp_enable, "")
| eval v_value=coalesce('vpValue', vpValue, vp_value, "")
| eval current_value=if(e_enable!="", tolower(trim(e_enable)), tolower(trim(v_value)))
| where current_value!="" AND isnotnull(current_value) AND isnotnull(token_name)
| stats latest(_time) as last_poll, latest(current_value) as current_value, values(dn) as inv_dns by server_mo, token_name
| join type=left max=0 server_mo
    [ search index=cisco_ucs sourcetype="cisco:ucs:inventory" earliest=-24h@h
      | search classId="lsServer"
      | where !match(coalesce(type, ""), "template$")
      | eval server_mo=coalesce(pnDn, pn_dn, "")
      | eval sp_name=coalesce(name, "")
      | eval sp_dn=coalesce(dn, "")
      | where isnotnull(server_mo) AND server_mo!=""
      | dedup server_mo
      | table server_mo, sp_name, sp_dn
    ]
| fillnull value="(unresolved profile in this poll window)" sp_name
| fillnull value="" sp_dn
| eval sp_tier=case(
    match(sp_name, "(?i)^HFT[-_]"), "hft",
    match(sp_name, "(?i)TRD[-]"), "trading",
    match(sp_name, "(?i)PROD[-]DB|DB[-]PROD"), "db_prod",
    1=1, "default"
  )
| lookup ucs_bios_policy_baseline sp_tier token_name OUTPUT expected_value, severity_if_drift, security_class, business_owner, change_ticket_required, policy_note
| eval norm_cur=lower(trim(current_value))
| eval norm_exp=lower(trim(expected_value))
| eval is_drift=if(isnotnull(expected_value) AND expected_value!="" AND norm_cur!=norm_exp, 1, 0)
| eval severity_bucket=case(
    is_drift=0, "ok",
    is_drift=1 AND (lower(severity_if_drift)="critical" OR match(token_name, "(?i)IntelVirtual|TrustedPlatform|Aes")), "critical",
    is_drift=1 AND (lower(severity_if_drift)="high" OR change_ticket_required="yes"), "high",
    is_drift=1 AND lower(severity_if_drift)="low", "low",
    is_drift=1, "medium",
    1=1, "info"
  )
| where is_drift=1
| where severity_bucket IN ("critical", "high", "medium", "low")
| sort 0 - last_poll, severity_bucket, server_mo, token_name
| table last_poll, severity_bucket, server_mo, sp_name, sp_dn, token_name, current_value, expected_value, norm_cur, norm_exp, inv_dns, sp_tier, security_class, business_owner, change_ticket_required, policy_note, severity_if_drift
description: Pulls the latest per-server `classId` token rows for biosUnit, biossettings, and every biosVf object your UCSM inventory stream exposes, normalises the MO value with `coalesce()` over `vpEnable` and `vpValue` spellings, derives the physical `server_mo` by trimming from `/bios` in `dn`, joins `cisco:ucs:inventory` `lsServer` on `pnDn=server_mo` to recover `sp_name`, maps a compact `sp_tier` in Splunk, and compares the live text to a peer-reviewed **ucs_bios_policy_baseline.csv** row so a wrong-BIOS posture is visible even when the service profile association story is still green. This is intentionally not `assocState` posture and not a FRU or adaptor firmware file check.
value: Security, compliance, and application owners care whether VT-x, TPM, AES, boot mode, memory RAS, and power-performance modes match the real risk profile of the workload, not just whether a profile is attached. A node can be associated and on golden firmware, yet be wrong for a confidential VM farm, a trading turret stack that needs SNC off, or a regulated encryption baseline that needs hardware features on. Catching that drift in Splunk gives you ticket-grade evidence, routes row-level ownership through `security_class` and `policy_note`, and keeps the BIOS review cadence outside the noisier LCM bundle tickets that own firmware file strings. Cross-check: for profile association posture first, use UC-19.1.2; for firmware file versions, UC-19.1.10; for vNIC mLOM firmware, UC-19.1.32—UC-19.1.17 is a different audience and a different audit beat.
implementation: Install `Splunk_TA_cisco_ucs` on a forwarder and search heads, enable inventory with BIOS and `lsServer` classes, add `lookups/ucs_bios_policy_baseline.csv` and `transforms.conf` in a wrapper app, save the `spl` as a report and a filtered alert on `severity_bucket` in the critical to medium band, and pass CSV to ticketing, not a raw page, unless a separate executive runbook already demands it.
visualization: (1) Table of the final `table` output for ticket paste. (2) Single value tiles: counts for `critical`, `high`, `medium`. (3) Column or bar of top `token_name` among drifts. (4) Stacked timechart of new drift by `sp_tier` during a campaign. (5) A narrow companion panel to UC-19.1.2 that only lists the same `sp_name` when `configState` is non-green, to separate posture from BIOS value drift.
cimSpl:
| tstats summariesonly=true count as inv from datamodel=Inventory
  where nodename=Inventory
  by _time | timechart count
| appendcols [
  search `comment("Optional CIM sense-check only — UCSM BIOS token fields are not a first-class Inventory.Configuration node. Keep the cisco:ucs:inventory path as system of record for vpEnable and vpValue.")` index=cisco_ucs sourcetype="cisco:ucs:inventory" earliest=-24h@h
  | head 0 ]
dataModelAcceleration: Accelerate the Inventory data model only after a deliberate CIM mapping for UCS; most teams keep the raw `cisco:ucs:inventory` search in production because biosVf token fields are not a stock Inventory.Configuration leaf. If you do map, re-run acceleration after any `Splunk_TA_cisco_ucs` upgrade that renames `vpValue`.
knownFalsePositives: A vendor BIOS bundle that changes factory defaults for a token can look like fleet-wide drift until your baseline CSV catches up with the new approved string, even if UCSM and every profile are healthy. A sustainability programme that flips Power profile to a savings class for a month will trip performance rules until the campaign row is in the lookup or a temporary tier is in place. Hyper-Threading is intentionally `disabled` on a handful of HFT- or PROD-DB- service profiles: encode those names in a tier map, not a single global enabled row. Sub-NUMA tuning diverges between a latency cohort and a general virtualisation pool; a one-line global expectation will misfire until you add `sp_tier` rows. AES-NI can read `disabled` for one poll right after a panicked CIMC or rollback event that was not a policy decision—treat as incident-correlated, not a steady finding. TPM `vpEnable` can lag a physical TPM install until the next inventory cycle—dedupe to `server_mo`+`token` with a 12–24h dwell before paging. A rebind to a new template that inherits a different child BIOS policy can show `pending-reboot` while the effective token is still the old one on metal—pair with the association UC, not a blind BIOS alert. If two BIOS policies in the same hierarchy disagree and the child wins, the child value is correct on the wire; a CSV line that only knows the parent will read as drift until the governance table matches the effective policy.

references.title: Cisco UCS Manager Server Management Guide, Release 4.2 (BIOS policy and service profile context)
Cisco UCS Manager XML API Reference, Release 4.2 (MIM: biosVf* MO classes)
Splunk documentation — Integrate data with Cisco UCS (supported add-on)

hardware: Cisco UCS B200 M5/M6/M7 blade servers, UCS C220 M5/M6/M7 and UCS C240 M5/M6/M7 rack servers, UCS C480 M5 where managed from UCSM, UCS X210c M6/M7 and UCS X410c M6 server nodes, paired with Cisco Fabric Interconnects in the 6300/6400/6500 families. Cross-walk the hardware tag your organisation uses in purchasing (for example `UCSC-C240-M7SX`, `UCSC-C220-M7S`, `UCSB-B200-M7`, `UCSX-210C-M7`) to the CIMC or board revision your BIOS token list is tied to, because a token default can move between board generations. The inventory MO tree under `sys/chassis-*/blade-*` and `sys/rack-unit-*` is the same anchor the SPL trims from each **biosVf** `dn` before it joins a profile; IMM-only C-series is not the primary path here.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites

• `Splunk_TA_cisco_ucs` ([Splunkbase 2731](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/2731)) 4.3.x on a Heavy Forwarder (or any host allowed to run modular inputs) and a copy on Search Heads for `lookup` definitions and field hints.
• A dedicated `cisco_ucs` index (or an org alias you reference consistently) with a read-only service account to UCS Manager that can read the inventory class list that includes biosUnit, biossettings, and biosVf families, and lsServer for association to `pnDn` bindings.
• A private wrapper app; never edit the vendor `default/` tree. Network path from the poller to the UCSM virtual IP is HTTPS `443` with full certificate trust, not a broken corporate proxy, or every poll is empty.
• A governance owner pair: the Compute Platform team for tokens with no direct security control meaning, and the Security team for the first three tokens your policy names (VT-x, TPM, AES) when those appear as biosVf classes in inventory.
• A baseline discipline: the CSV in this use case is the contract; every campaign that changes a BIOS token in UCSM is mirrored in the CSV the same day the change window is approved, not the week after someone asks in chat.

**Step 1 — Configure data collection, transforms, and the baseline file**

(1) Install the add-on from Splunkbase 2731. In `local/inputs.conf`, enable the same Cisco UCS inventory modular input family the TA ships. Every boolean key, stanza name, and account handle must be copied from `default/inputs.conf` for your 4.3.x build; the shape below is intentionally descriptive, not a fork of a parameter name the TA does not list.

(2) Keep sourcetype on the add-on’s inventory tag `cisco:ucs:inventory`. If an older runbook still references a standalone cisco:ucs:bios string, that path is a fiction for this add-on: BIOS tokens arrive as their own `classId` values on the same sourcetype. The inventory poll class list in the UI or config must still include the BIOS VFs your hardware exposes; a minimal poll that only sees chassis fans will not show biosVf token rows, and the search below is empty for the wrong reason.

(3) Example `inputs.conf` fragment you merge into a real vendor stanza after you reconcile keys:

```ini
# $SPLUNK_HOME/etc/apps/Splunk_TA_cisco_ucs/local/inputs.conf
# Merge into the ciscoucs (or current) stanza the TA documents.

# disabled = 0
# index = cisco_ucs
# url = https://<ucsm-fabric-interconnect-vip>
# interval = 300
# (credential reference exactly as the TA UI and default stanza name)

# The inventory or class list must still resolve biosUnit, biosSettings, the biosVf* token classes you chose to govern,
# and lsServer. If the TA offers separate intervals, keep the inventory leg at the tighter of 300-900 seconds
# until a steady-state volume study says you can slow down.
```

(4) `transforms.conf` in your wrapper app:

```ini
[ucs_bios_policy_baseline]
filename = ucs_bios_policy_baseline.csv
case_sensitive_match = false
min_matches = 1
```

Reload the bundle on the search head, then open **Settings** → **Lookups** → **Lookup table files** and grant read to a role like `bios_compliance_reader`, not `admin` only.

(5) The baseline CSV is UTF-8, peer-reviewed in git, and version-tagged. Header row and eight campaign rows to seed your shop (extend with your true profile prefixes and tokens). Governance tables often list `sp_name_pattern` (glob such as `*` or `HFT-*`), `token_name`, `expected_value`, `severity_if_drift`, `security_class`, `business_owner`, `change_ticket_required`, and optional `policy_note`. The paste-runnable SPL below uses derived `sp_tier` plus `token_name` as lookup keys to keep the fence small; map `sp_name` to `sp_tier` with the inline `case()` or add `lookups/ucs_sp_tier_map.csv` when your source of truth is `sp_name_pattern` rows instead of tier short names.

sp_tier,token_name,expected_value,severity_if_drift,security_class,business_owner,change_ticket_required,policy_note
default,biosVfIntelVirtualizationTechnology,enabled,critical,platform_security,secops-contacts@org.example,yes,Hypervisor and confidential VM class must expose VT
default,biosVfTrustedPlatformModuleSupport,enabled,high,platform_security,secops-contacts@org.example,yes,TPM required for at-rest and boot posture evidence
default,biosVfPwrPerfTuning,os,medium,power,platform-ops@org.example,optional,OS governs P-states; align to sustainability policy separately
hft,biosVfHyperThreading,disabled,low,workload,trading-owners@org.example,optional,Determinism and cache isolation for trading tier
trading,biosVfSubNumaCluster,disabled,low,workload,trading-owners@org.example,optional,HFT SNC posture; general prod may use default
default,biosVfBootMode,uefi,high,boot,platform-ops@org.example,yes,Secure UEFI and measured boot class
default,biosVfAesNi,enabled,critical,platform_security,secops-contacts@org.example,yes,Hardware crypto baseline for in-node encryption
default,biosVfMemoryRas,mirror,high,resiliency,platform-ops@org.example,yes,Residency-class node DIMM resiliency
default,biosVfPatrolScrub,enabled,medium,resiliency,platform-ops@org.example,optional,Memory patrol for long-run banks

(6) `sp_tier` is derived in SPL with a short `case()` on `sp_name` so a trading prefix can differ from a general population without splintering the entire fleet into duplicate searches. A mature alternative is a second table `lookups/ucs_sp_tier_map.csv` with one row per `sp_name` and an explicit `sp_tier` column, then a `lookup` before the baseline. When two BIOS policy objects inherit the same biosVf with different end values, the child policy in UCSM wins on the wire; the CSV has to name the end state you want after inheritance, or you will get false "drift" with an honest config.

(7) Expected values use the same string discipline UCSM returns through `vpValue` and `vpEnable` after the add-on normalises. If a token prints `platform-performance` in your lab, put that exact string in the CSV, not a different marketing synonym.

(8) Educational note: if you find an old cisco:ucs:bios string in a template, that sourcetype is not part of the canonical add-on emit list. Keep every BIOS signal on `cisco:ucs:inventory` and filter on `classId` and `dn`, not on a made-up second sourcetype.

Document `policy_note` for auditors; the column is the human contract that a dry token name cannot carry.

**Step 2 — Create the search, report, and alert**

Paste the `spl` field in Search and save it as a saved report `uc_19_1_17_bios_token_compliance`. The fence below must match the top-level `spl` field in this use case file byte for byte, including the leading comment macro line.

```spl
`comment("UC-19.1.17 — BIOS Policy Compliance (Cisco UCS): per-server per-token biosUnit/biossettings/biosVf* INVENTORY MO values vs ucs_bios_policy_baseline, joined to lsServer for sp_name. Educational: a templated cisco:ucs:bios sourcetype is not shipped in Splunk_TA_cisco_ucs — emit BIOS through cisco:ucs:inventory and classId.")`
index=cisco_ucs sourcetype="cisco:ucs:inventory" earliest=-24h@h
| eval class_id=lower(coalesce('classId', classId, ""))
| where class_id="biosunit" OR class_id="biossettings" OR like(class_id, "biosvf%")
| where isnotnull(dn) AND match(dn, "^sys/")
| eval server_mo=if(match(dn, "/[Bb]ios"), replace(dn, "/[Bb]ios.*$", ""), null())
| where isnotnull(server_mo) AND server_mo!=""
| eval token_name=coalesce('classId', classId, "")
| eval e_enable=coalesce('vpEnable', vpEnable, vp_enable, "")
| eval v_value=coalesce('vpValue', vpValue, vp_value, "")
| eval current_value=if(e_enable!="", tolower(trim(e_enable)), tolower(trim(v_value)))
| where current_value!="" AND isnotnull(current_value) AND isnotnull(token_name)
| stats latest(_time) as last_poll, latest(current_value) as current_value, values(dn) as inv_dns by server_mo, token_name
| join type=left max=0 server_mo
    [ search index=cisco_ucs sourcetype="cisco:ucs:inventory" earliest=-24h@h
      | search classId="lsServer"
      | where !match(coalesce(type, ""), "template$")
      | eval server_mo=coalesce(pnDn, pn_dn, "")
      | eval sp_name=coalesce(name, "")
      | eval sp_dn=coalesce(dn, "")
      | where isnotnull(server_mo) AND server_mo!=""
      | dedup server_mo
      | table server_mo, sp_name, sp_dn
    ]
| fillnull value="(unresolved profile in this poll window)" sp_name
| fillnull value="" sp_dn
| eval sp_tier=case(
    match(sp_name, "(?i)^HFT[-_]"), "hft",
    match(sp_name, "(?i)TRD[-]"), "trading",
    match(sp_name, "(?i)PROD[-]DB|DB[-]PROD"), "db_prod",
    1=1, "default"
  )
| lookup ucs_bios_policy_baseline sp_tier token_name OUTPUT expected_value, severity_if_drift, security_class, business_owner, change_ticket_required, policy_note
| eval norm_cur=lower(trim(current_value))
| eval norm_exp=lower(trim(expected_value))
| eval is_drift=if(isnotnull(expected_value) AND expected_value!="" AND norm_cur!=norm_exp, 1, 0)
| eval severity_bucket=case(
    is_drift=0, "ok",
    is_drift=1 AND (lower(severity_if_drift)="critical" OR match(token_name, "(?i)IntelVirtual|TrustedPlatform|Aes")), "critical",
    is_drift=1 AND (lower(severity_if_drift)="high" OR change_ticket_required="yes"), "high",
    is_drift=1 AND lower(severity_if_drift)="low", "low",
    is_drift=1, "medium",
    1=1, "info"
  )
| where is_drift=1
| where severity_bucket IN ("critical", "high", "medium", "low")
| sort 0 - last_poll, severity_bucket, server_mo, token_name
| table last_poll, severity_bucket, server_mo, sp_name, sp_dn, token_name, current_value, expected_value, norm_cur, norm_exp, inv_dns, sp_tier, security_class, business_owner, change_ticket_required, policy_note, severity_if_drift
```

Alerting cadence: run daily or every six hours in large estates; route critical to Security-owned queues, high to Platform with an attached change id when `change_ticket_required` is `yes`, and keep low in a work queue that never auto-pages. The primary action is CSV to ticketing with the full row, not a product-supplied PagerDuty auto-page for BIOS drift on its own.

**Step 3 — Validate in Cisco UCS Manager**

Open UCSM and cross-check a row Splunk says is critical or high. Use Server and pick the Servers or Rack-Mounts / Servers path that matches the `server_mo` string (for B-series, expand Chassis first; for C-series, Rack Units). The authoritative BIOS policy for the service profile is attached under the profile’s Policies and BIOS policy, while a read-only, effective value list is also visible in **Server** → your **Server** object → **Inventory** → the BIOS or equivalent panel that shows live token text. If you are on a 4.2-generation guide page, the Server Management user guide is the same chapter family the references list cites; step names move slightly in later dot releases, but the profile-level BIOS object and the server inventory read-out still pair-check each other. If the GUI shows a `pending-reboot` banner, wait for one inventory period before you file a long-lived defect, because a template rebind or in-place BIOS policy change is expected to look yellow briefly.

**Step 4 — Operationalize in Splunk**

Build a "BIOS token posture" dashboard: row 1, single values for `critical`, `high`, and `medium` counts. Row 2, a table that matches the `table` stage in the primary search. Row 3, a time series of new drift by `sp_tier` to show campaign quality. Add a `bios_compliance_reader` role that can run the report and read `lookups/ucs_bios_policy_baseline.csv`, not write admin objects. The Compute Platform team owns the rows where `security_class` is not `platform_security`; the Security team owns the VT, TPM, and AES biosVf family rows. The alert post-action is CSV to ticketing with the type your organisation already uses for configuration drift, not a lights-out PagerDuty trigger.

**Step 5 — Troubleshooting and failure-mode catalogue**

1. Wrong index — you pointed the modular input to a test index, but the saved search still reads `index=cisco_ucs`. Fix the input destination or align every `index=` in consumers.

2. No biosVf* rows, only a handful of chassis objects — the inventory class list in the add-on is still trimmed to a cheap subset, or the poll interval is so long the search looks empty between passes. Compare `_time` spread on `cisco:ucs:inventory` with the configured interval, then re-enable the MO classes the TA documents for biosVf tokens.

3. Read-only user RBAC hides a subtree the GUI admin can still see — Splunk will miss entire `lsServer` and `pnDn` sets; the profile join fails and the table shows the unresolved profile string. Widen the API role or narrow the search to the subtree the read-only account actually owns.

4. Baseline key mismatch — `sp_tier` from `case(sp_name, ...)` is too coarse, or a `token_name` in the CSV does not match the `classId` string coming off inventory (Cisco is case-faithful; one edited letter breaks the `lookup`).

5. Inheritance surprise — two policies stack and the child wins in UCSM while the CSV still encodes the parent token; update the expected string to the effective value or add a `policy_note` and a template-keyed key if you outgrow the two-column lookup.

6. Field-name drift after an add-on upgrade — if `vpValue` reappears with a new spelling, add it to the `coalesce` list; never hand-edit `_raw` in `props` without a parser review and a time-boxed A/B in Search.

7. Pending states — `applying` and `pending-reboot` on the `lsServer` path make good servers look non-green for a window. Pair with UC-19.1.2 and mute only with a campaign-specific lookup, not a silent blanket drop.

8. Clock skew — heavy forwarder time wrong by an hour still lands events, but a mis-set `earliest` window in a manually saved alert can make the last-24h window look empty. Compare `_indextime` to `_time` on a known row.

9. Template rows in inventory — the join subsearch already excludes `lsServer` `type` values that end in `template`; re-open one mis-filtered name if a lab reuses odd strings.

10. Unicode or CSV line endings in the baseline — a UTF-8 BOM on the first row can make the first `token_name` unjoinable. Strip BOM, normalise to UNIX newlines, and re-upload the lookup file.

**Cross-refs and sibling UCs**

If you want overall lsServer posture first, use UC-19.1.2. If you want per-FRU firmware file strings, use UC-19.1.10. UC-19.1.32 covers CNA adapter firmware drift on the mLOM and mezz path, not the BIOS token path. A server can be green on association and on FRU and adapter firmware, yet off on a biosVf token—this is the security and application-performance check with its own review cadence. Intersight-primary profiles that are not represented in a UCSM inventory stream you index are a different data path: UC-19.1.20 and UC-19.1.21 are the bundle and HCL hygiene cousins when UCSM is not the system of record.
grandmaExplanation: We watch the real low-level power, boot, and security switches on each server and compare the live set to the rules your teams wrote, even when a machine already passes a higher-level applied profile check. We send a short, clear list to the right owner without mixing this with checks for main firmware files or add-in network card code.
